article_title: Back-to-Back Jury Verdicts Find Meta Negligent and Liable for Child Safety Failures as More Than 2,000 Lawsuits Await

findings.message: Source fetched via Archive.org fallback (original returned 200)

content_preview.title: Back-to-Back Jury Verdicts Find Meta Negligent and Liable for Child Safety Failures as More Than 2,000 Lawsuits Await
content_preview.summary: A New Mexico jury ordered Meta to pay $375 million for enabling child exploitation, and a California bellwether jury found both Meta and YouTube negligent in designing addictive platforms that harmed a minor.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Two jury verdicts handed down within 48 hours have opened a new legal front against Meta Platforms over the safety of children on its products. On March 24, a Santa Fe jury ordered Meta to pay $375 million in civil penalties for violating New Mexico's consumer protection law by concealing evidence of child sexual exploitation on Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp, according to [NBC News](https://www.nbcnews.com/tech/social-media/jury-orders-meta-pay-375-million-new-mexico-lawsuit-chi...

editor_notes.content_quality: Comprehensive coverage of two landmark verdicts with clear structure. Good cross-reference to existing congressional children's safety article. 738 words appropriate for News. 6 diverse sources from major outlets.
editor_notes.source_verification: NBC News confirmed: $375M verdict, NM consumer protection law, AG Torrez undercover operation, fake accounts under 14, sexually explicit material, Meta appeal. Fortune confirmed: $381M combined, $201B revenue context, 40+ state AG suits, tens of billions sought, Section 230 defense, appeal plans. CNBC returned 403 but California verdict details ($6M, 70/30 split) corroborated by Fortune. ABC News and Axios not directly fetched but confirmed via corroborating sources.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All major claims verified. $375M NM verdict, $6M CA verdict, March 24-25 dates, bellwether designation, 2000+ lawsuits, Torrez quote, plaintiff details, Section 230 defense all confirmed across multiple sources.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and balanced. Presents both the plaintiffs' arguments and Meta's position/appeal plans. Appropriate use of 'What We Don't Know' section. Good editorial link to congressional legislation article.
editor_notes.originality: No prior Meta child safety verdict coverage. Related to existing congressional safety legislation article (March 23) but covers different event.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Excellent legal reporting with strong multi-source backing. All key figures and dates verified. Balanced treatment of a sensitive topic. Ready for publication.
